subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 4. In this unit you will become fluent and confident with decimals – the numbers that carry a decimal point, such as 3.75, 0.6 and 12.045. You already meet decimals every day, often without noticing. When Amina counts out 147.50 shillings in change at the Hargeisa market, when Yusuf times his 100 metre sprint at 13.8 seconds, or when a trader in Mogadishu weighs 2.5 kilograms of bariis rice, they are all working with decimals. This year we go further than ever before: you will master all four operations with decimals, multiply and divide them with precision, convert smoothly between decimals and fractions, and solve multi-step real-world problems.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 8 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work every example yourself on paper before you look at the solution, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a different way, or check your working. Your teacher will meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels difficult. There is no rush – accuracy matters far more than speed.

Why do decimals matter so much? They are the language of money, measurement and science. A shopkeeper who cannot add prices accurately loses money. A builder who misreads 2.5 metres as 2.05 metres cuts the wrong length of wood. A nurse measuring medicine, an engineer designing a bridge, a footballer's coach recording sprint times – all depend on precise decimal arithmetic. When you finish this unit you will hold a skill you will use for the rest of your life, and you will have built the foundation you need for percentages, ratio, and algebra in the units ahead.

As you work, keep a pencil and paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Line up the decimal points, carry out the multiplication, count the decimal places, and check your answer with an estimate. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is a signpost showing exactly what to practise next. Let us begin.
Explain the place value of every digit in a decimal, including tenths, hundredths and thousandths.
Compare and order decimals accurately, even when they have different numbers of decimal places.
Add and subtract decimals by lining up the decimal points.
Multiply decimals, placing the decimal point correctly by counting decimal places.
Divide decimals, including dividing by a decimal, by converting to whole numbers.
Multiply and divide decimals by powers of 10 (10, 100, 1000) using the decimal-shift shortcut.
Convert fluently between fractions and decimals, and recognise terminating and recurring decimals.

concepts.title: Decimal Place Value
concepts.explanation: Everything in this unit rests on one big idea: place value continues past the decimal point. To the left of the point you have the familiar places – ones, tens, hundreds. Each time you move one place to the left, the value is ten times bigger. Each time you move one place to the right, the value is ten times smaller. This pattern does not stop at the ones column. It simply keeps going, and the decimal point marks the boundary. The first place after the point is tenths (each worth 1/10 = 0.1). The next is…
concepts.example: Understanding place value lets you read any decimal correctly. In 0.0456, there is a 0 in the tenths place, a 4 in the hundredths place, a 5 in the thousandths place, and a 6 in the ten-thousandths place. The zero…

concepts.title: Comparing and Ordering Decimals
concepts.explanation: To compare two decimals, examine their place values from left to right, starting with the largest place. Move rightwards only when the digits you are looking at are equal. The first place where the digits differ decides which number is greater. Compare 3.456 and 3.465. The ones are equal (3 = 3). The tenths are equal (4 = 4). Move to the hundredths: 5 versus 6. Since 5 < 6, we conclude 3.456 < 3.465. We did not need to look at the thousandths at all, because the decision was already made.
concepts.example: The most common trap in the whole unit is thinking a decimal is bigger just because it has more digits. Compare 0.7 and 0.68. Some students say 0.68 is bigger because 68 is bigger than 7 – but that ignores place value.…

concepts.title: Adding and Subtracting Decimals
concepts.explanation: Adding and subtracting decimals uses one golden rule: line up the decimal points. Digits may only be combined with digits in the same place value column, so the points must sit directly under one another. Filling empty places with trailing zeros keeps the columns straight. To add 23.456 + 7.89, stack the numbers so the points align and add a trailing zero to 7.89 so both have three decimal places:
concepts.example: Add column by column from the right, carrying whenever a column reaches ten, exactly as with whole numbers. The decimal point in the answer drops straight down from the points above it.

concepts.title: Multiplying Decimals
concepts.explanation: Multiplying decimals is easier than it looks, because you can ignore the decimal points at first. The method has three steps: multiply as whole numbers, count the decimal places, then place the point. The number of decimal places in the answer equals the total number of decimal places in the two numbers you multiplied. Work out 2.3 × 1.4. Ignore the points and multiply 23 × 14 = 322. Now count decimal places: 2.3 has one, 1.4 has one, so the answer needs two decimal places. Place the point: 3.22. Check with an…
concepts.example: Try a harder one: 3.45 × 2.6. Multiply 345 × 26 = 8970. Count decimal places: 3.45 has two, 2.6 has one, giving three decimal places, so the answer is 8.970, which we write as 8.97. Notice how the trailing zero was…

concepts.title: Dividing Decimals
concepts.explanation: Division of decimals splits into two cases. The first, and simpler, is dividing a decimal by a whole number. Divide as normal and keep the decimal point in the answer directly above the point in the number being divided. For example, 12.6 ÷ 3 = 4.2: the point in 4.2 sits above the point in 12.6. The second case is dividing by a decimal, which needs one clever move. You cannot easily divide by 0.3, so first turn the divisor into a whole number. Multiply both numbers by the same power of 10, which keeps the answer…
concepts.example: Why does multiplying both numbers by 10 leave the answer the same? Because a division is really a fraction, and multiplying the top and bottom of a fraction by the same amount gives an equivalent fraction. 8.4 ÷ 0.3 is…

concepts.title: Converting Between Fractions and Decimals
concepts.explanation: Fractions and decimals are two ways of writing the same thing, and being able to swap between them freely is a powerful skill. To turn a fraction into a decimal, divide the numerator by the denominator. The fraction 3/4 becomes 3 ÷ 4 = 0.75. The fraction 5/8 becomes 5 ÷ 8 = 0.625. The fraction 3/5 becomes 3 ÷ 5 = 0.6. This division gives one of two results. A terminating decimal stops, like 0.75. A recurring (repeating) decimal goes on forever with a repeating pattern, like 1/3 = 0.333… or 1/7 = 0.142857142857….…
concepts.example: To turn a terminating decimal into a fraction, use place value to name it, then simplify. The decimal 0.375 is read as 375 thousandths, so it is 375/1000. Divide the top and bottom by their greatest common factor, 125,…

explorations.answer: 1) a) 5 hundredths = 0.05, b) 6 tenths = 0.6, c) 9 thousandths = 0.009.

methods.title: How to multiply two decimals
methods.example: In the number 67.459, what is the value of the digit 5?
steps: Ignore the decimal points and multiply as whole numbers.
Count the decimal places in each original number and add them.
Put that many decimal places into your answer.
Estimate to check the point is in the right place.

methods.title: How to divide by a decimal
steps: Multiply the divisor by 10, 100 or 1000 to make it a whole number.
Multiply the dividend by the same amount.
Divide as usual, keeping the decimal point lined up.
Estimate to check the answer is sensible.

methods.title: How to convert a fraction to a decimal
steps: Divide the numerator (top) by the denominator (bottom).
If it stops, it is a terminating decimal.
If a digit or block repeats forever, it is a recurring decimal.

methods.title: Multiplying decimals
steps: Solution: Ignore the points: 26 × 45 = 1170.
Count decimal places: 2.6 has one and 4.5 has one, giving two decimal places.
Estimate: 3 × 4 = 12, which confirms the answer.

methods.title: A clever multiplication
steps: Solution: Multiply as whole numbers: 125 × 16 = 2000.
Count decimal places: 0.125 has three, 16 has none, so three decimal places.
(You could also spot that 0.125 = 1/8, and 16 ÷ 8 = 2.)

methods.title: Dividing by a decimal
steps: Solution: Make the divisor whole.
Multiply both numbers by 10: 7.2 ÷ 0.9 becomes 72 ÷ 9 = 8.
Estimate to check: 7 ÷ 1 = 7, close to 8, so it is sensible.

workedExamples.title: Place value of a digit
workedExamples.prompt: In the number 67.459, what is the value of the digit 5?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: After the decimal point the places are tenths, then hundredths, then thousandths. The digits after the point are 4 (tenths), 5 (hundredths), 9 (thousandths). So the 5 is in the hundredths place and its value is 5 × 0.01 = 0.05.

workedExamples.title: Ordering decimals
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Give each three decimal places: 0.340, 0.304, 0.430, 0.034. Compare from the left. Smallest is 0.034, then 0.304, then 0.340, then 0.430. So the order is 0.034, 0.304, 0.34, 0.43.

workedExamples.title: Subtracting from a whole number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Write 25 as 25.000 so both numbers have three decimal places. Line up the points and subtract: 25.000 − 12.345 = 12.655. Estimate to check: 25 − 12 = 13, close to 12.655, so it is reasonable.

workedExamples.title: Multiplying decimals
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ignore the points: 26 × 45 = 1170. Count decimal places: 2.6 has one and 4.5 has one, giving two decimal places. So 1170 becomes 11.70 = 11.7. Estimate: 3 × 4 = 12, which confirms the answer.

workedExamples.title: A clever multiplication
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiply as whole numbers: 125 × 16 = 2000. Count decimal places: 0.125 has three, 16 has none, so three decimal places. 2000 becomes 2.000 = 2. (You could also spot that 0.125 = 1/8, and 16 ÷ 8 = 2.)

workedExamples.title: Dividing by a decimal
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Make the divisor whole. Multiply both numbers by 10: 7.2 ÷ 0.9 becomes 72 ÷ 9 = 8. Estimate to check: 7 ÷ 1 = 7, close to 8, so it is sensible.

workedExamples.title: Fraction to decimal
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divide the numerator by the denominator: 7 ÷ 8 = 0.875. Because the denominator 8 = 2 × 2 × 2 has only 2s as factors, the decimal terminates – exactly as we predicted.

workedExamples.title: A money word problem
workedExamples.prompt: Sahra buys items costing $15.67, $9.89 and $12.45. She pays with a $50 note. How much change does she get?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First add the costs: 15.67 + 9.89 + 12.45 = $38.01. Then subtract from 50: 50.00 − 38.01 = $11.99 change. Estimate: 16 + 10 + 12 = 38, and 50 − 38 = 12, close to $11.99, so it checks out.

workedExamples.title: Cost per kilogram
workedExamples.prompt: If 3.5 kg of apples costs 10.50 shillings, what is the cost per kilogram?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divide the total cost by the weight: 10.50 ÷ 3.5. Multiply both by 10: 105 ÷ 35 = 3. So the apples cost 3 shillings per kilogram.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangle is 6.5 cm long and 3.8 cm wide. Find its area and its perimeter.

activities.title: Receipt Detective
activities.materials: a real shopping receipt (or write out five prices yourself), paper and pencil.
steps: Copy down five prices from the receipt, each with two decimal places.
Estimate the total by rounding each price to the nearest whole unit and adding.
Now add the exact prices, lining up the decimal points carefully.
Compare your estimate with the exact total. How close were you?
Work out the change from a round amount (for example, 100 shillings or $50).

activities.title: Kitchen Measuring and Halving
activities.materials: a measuring cup or jug, water, and a simple recipe.
steps: Measure amounts such as 2.5 cups, 0.75 cups and 1.25 cups of water.
Write each amount as a fraction as well (2.5 = 2 1/2, 0.75 = 3/4, 1.25 = 1 1/4).
Halve the whole recipe by dividing each amount by 2.
Now make 1.5 times the recipe by multiplying each amount by 1.5.
Check: does doubling your 'half recipe' bring you back to the original amounts?

activities.title: The Decimal Place-Value Game
activities.materials: paper cards with the digits 0–9, and a home-made place value board (ones, point, tenths, hundredths, thousandths).
steps: Shuffle the digit cards and place them face down.
Each player draws four cards and arranges them on the board to make a decimal.
The player with the LARGER decimal wins the round – prove it by comparing place by place.
Play again, but this time the SMALLER decimal wins.
Talk about the strategy: where should you place a large digit to make the biggest number?

activities.title: Best-Value Market Investigation
activities.materials: prices and weights of a few items (real or invented) from the market.
steps: Choose two packet sizes of the same food, for example 2.5 kg of rice and 1.5 kg of rice, each with a price.
For each packet, divide the price by the weight to find the cost per kilogram.
Compare the two cost-per-kilogram figures to decide which is better value.
Repeat with dates, sugar or camel milk sold in different sizes.
Write one sentence explaining how dividing decimals helps a family spend wisely.

activities.title: Stopwatch Sprint Times (Family or Group)
activities.materials: a phone stopwatch and a safe space to run or walk.
steps: Time each person over a short distance, recording seconds to two decimal places (for example 12.47 s).
Order everyone's times from fastest to slowest by comparing decimals carefully.
Find the difference between the fastest and slowest time by subtracting decimals.
Add all the times together to find a 'team total'.
Try again after practice – did your times improve? By how many seconds?

activities.title: Fraction-Decimal Matching Pairs
activities.materials: paper to make a set of matching cards.
steps: Make cards for common fractions: 1/2, 1/4, 3/4, 1/5, 1/8, 3/8, 5/8.
Make matching cards for their decimals: 0.5, 0.25, 0.75, 0.2, 0.125, 0.375, 0.625.
Shuffle all cards face down and play a memory-matching game: turn over two and keep them if they are a pair.
For each pair you find, say aloud how you would convert one to the other by dividing.
Challenge: add a card for a recurring decimal such as 1/3 = 0.333… and discuss why it never ends.

rules.title: Place Value Rule
rules.text: After the decimal point the places are tenths, hundredths, thousandths. 0.4 = 4 tenths; 0.07 = 7 hundredths; 0.009 = 9 thousandths. Trailing zeros do not change the value: 0.5 = 0.50 = 0.500.

rules.title: Add and Subtract Rule
rules.text: Line up the decimal points, one under the other. Fill empty places with trailing zeros so both numbers have the same number of decimal places. Add or subtract as normal; the point in the answer drops straight down.

rules.title: Multiply Rule
rules.text: Multiply the digits as whole numbers, ignoring the points. Count the total decimal places in both numbers. The answer has that many decimal places.

rules.title: Divide Rule
rules.text: To divide by a decimal, multiply both numbers by 10, 100 or 1000 until the divisor is whole. Then divide as usual. Example: 8.4 divided by 0.3 becomes 84 divided by 3 = 28.

rules.title: Powers of 10 Rule
rules.text: Multiply by 10, 100, 1000: move the point 1, 2, 3 places RIGHT. Divide by 10, 100, 1000: move the point 1, 2, 3 places LEFT.

terms: Dot separating whole numbers from parts
The first three places after the point
How many digits come after the point
A zero at the end that does not change value
A decimal that repeats forever, like 0.333…
commonMistakes: Stack the decimal points; use trailing zeros
Wrong decimal places in a product
Total the decimal places of both numbers
Shift both numbers by the same power of 10
Keep the zero straight after the point

realProblems.prompt: Khadija shares 4.5 kg of bariis equally among 6 families for iftar. How much does each family receive, to two decimal places?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A camel-milk seller pours 15.6 litres into 1.2-litre bottles. How many full bottles, and how much milk remains?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan runs three laps in 13.8 s, 14.25 s and 13.96 s. Find his total time and his fastest lap.

realProblems.prompt: Omar tracks a small budget: income 245.60 shillings, spending 87.50 on food, 42.30 on transport and 15.75 on charity (sadaqah). How much is left?

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A 18.6-metre rope is cut into 1.2-metre pieces. How many whole pieces, and how much is left over?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 18.6 ÷ 1.2 = 15.5, so 15 whole pieces with 0.6 m left over.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Convert 0.333… (recurring) to a fraction, showing the algebra: let x = 0.333…, then 10x = 3.333….
Amina has 12.567 g, 8.789 g and 15.234 g of three spices. Order them lightest to heaviest, then find the total mass.
Which is the better value: 2.5 kg of dates for 30 shillings, or 1.5 kg for 19.50 shillings? Show cost per kilogram.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: First option 30 ÷ 2.5 = 12 shillings/kg; second 19.50 ÷ 1.5 = 13 shillings/kg; the 2.5 kg pack is better value.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Converting Between Fractions and Decimals.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Fractions and decimals are two ways of writing the same thing, and being able to swap between them freely is a powerful skill. To turn a fraction into a decimal, divide the numerator by the denominator. The fraction 3/4 becomes 3 ÷ 4 = 0.75. The fraction 5/8 becomes 5 ÷ 8 = 0.625. The fraction 3/5 becomes 3 ÷ 5 = 0.6. This division gives one of two results. A terminating decimal stops, like 0.75. A recurring (repeating) decimal goes on forever with a repeating pattern, like 1/3 = 0.333… or 1/7 = 0.142857142857….…

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work every example yourself on paper before you look at the solution, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a different way, or check your working. Your teacher will meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels difficult. There is no rush – accuracy matters far more than speed.

I can why do decimals matter so much? They are the language of money, measurement and science. A shopkeeper who cannot add prices accurately loses money. A builder who misreads 2.5 metres as 2.05 metres cuts the wrong length of wood. A nurse measuring medicine, an engineer designing a bridge, a footballer's coach recording sprint times – all depend on precise decimal arithmetic. When you finish this unit you will hold a skill you will use for the rest of your life, and you will have built the foundation you need for percentages, ratio, and algebra in the units ahead.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Line up the decimal points, carry out the multiplication, count the decimal places, and check your answer with an estimate. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is a signpost showing exactly what to practise next. Let us begin.
I can explain the place value of every digit in a decimal, including tenths, hundredths and thousandths.
I can compare and order decimals accurately, even when they have different numbers of decimal places.
I can add and subtract decimals by lining up the decimal points.
I can multiply decimals, placing the decimal point correctly by counting decimal places.
I can divide decimals, including dividing by a decimal, by converting to whole numbers.
